title: Psalms 110
subtitle: The LORD Says to My Lord: Sit at My Right Hand

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “Sit at My Right Hand Until I Make Your Enemies a Footstool”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD (YHWH) says to my lord (adoni): ‘Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet’" (v.1). THE most-quoted OT verse in the NT. Jesus uses it to silence the Pharisees (Matt 22:41–46): if David calls the Messiah "lord," how can the Messiah be merely David’s son? Peter quotes it at Pentecost (Acts 2:34–35). Paul quotes it for Christ’s exaltation (1 Cor 15:25; Eph 1:20; Col 3:1). Hebrews quotes it seven times. The psalm establishes Christ’s session: seated at God’s right hand, ruling until every enemy is subdued. "You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek" (v.4) — Hebrews 5–7 develops this as Christ’s eternal priesthood.

notes.note: "The LORD says to my lord: Sit at my right hand." The psalm’s opening is the NT’s most-quoted verse. Two lords: YHWH speaks to David’s lord (the Messiah). The session: seated at God’s right hand. The duration: until enemies become footstool. Jesus uses this to prove the Messiah is more than David’s son.

"The LORD will extend your mighty sceptre from Zion. Your troops will be willing on your day of battle." The Messiah rules from Zion. His army volunteers. The dew of youth (v.3): the Messiah’s followers are fresh and numerous as morning dew.

"The LORD has sworn and will not change his mind: ‘You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek.’" The dual office: king (v.1) AND priest (v.4). No Davidic king was also a priest. Only Christ fulfils both. The Melchizedek priesthood: pre-Aaronic, eternal, superior. Hebrews 5–7 builds its entire argument on this verse.

refs.note: Jesus uses this psalm to challenge Pharisees about how David's Lord can be his son.
Peter quotes this psalm to prove Jesus's resurrection and exaltation to God's right hand.
Hebrews quotes verse 1 as supremely applicable to the Son, not angels.

hist.context: Psalm 110 is the most-quoted psalm in the New Testament. The oracle 'the LORD says to my Lord' presents YHWH addressing another figure whom David calls 'my Lord.' The invitation to sit at God's right hand until enemies become a footstool establishes royal co-regency with divine authority.

sections.header: Verses 5–7 — “He Will Crush Kings on the Day of His Wrath”

heb.gloss: he will judge / he will execute judgment
heb.paragraph: "The Lord is at your right hand; he will crush kings on the day of his wrath. He will judge (yishpōṭ) the nations" (vv.5–6). The Messiah in battle: crushing kings, judging nations, filling the land with the dead. "He will drink from a brook along the way, and so he will lift his head" (v.7). The brook-drinking warrior: refreshed mid-battle, he lifts his head in victory. Seven verses. The most quoted, most christological, most theologically dense psalm in the Psalter.

notes.note: "The Lord is at your right hand; he will crush kings on the day of his wrath. He will judge the nations." The battle scene: the Messiah-king-priest conquers in God’s wrath. The day of wrath is the day of victory.

"He will drink from a brook along the way, and so he will lift his head." The warrior drinks — a moment of refreshment amid conquest. Then the head lifts: triumph. The image is of unstoppable advance, briefly pausing to drink, then pressing on to victory.

refs.note: Christ as priest forever in the order of Melchizedek is the key christological application.
Extended meditation on Melchizedekian priesthood develops the psalm's cryptic oracle.
Melchizedek blessing Abraham establishes the priesthood the psalm invokes.

hist.context: The divine oath 'you are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek' (v.4) combines royal and priestly roles — unusual since Israelite kings were not priests. Melchizedek, the mysterious priest-king of Salem who blessed Abraham, provides precedent. Hebrews argues this priesthood supersedes Levitical priesthood, making Christ both king and high priest.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe will judge the nations, heaping up the dead and crushing the rulers of the whole earth.ESVHe will execute judgment among the nations, filling them with corpses; he will shatter chiefs over the wide earth.NIVThe Lord has sworn and will not change his mind: “You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek.”ESVThe LORD has sworn and will not change his mind, \u201cYou are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek.\u201d

rows.label: The Conquest — 110:5–7
rows.text: "He will CRUSH KINGS." Judge the nations. Drink from a brook. Lift his head. Seven verses of density.

pairs.label: A
pairs.top: The LORD says to my lord: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet (v.1)
pairs.bottom: The Lord is at your right hand; he will crush kings on the day of his wrath. He will judge the nations (vv.5–6)

pairs.label: B
pairs.top: The LORD will extend your mighty scepter from Zion, saying: Rule in the midst of your enemies (v.2)
pairs.bottom: He will drink from a brook along the way and so will lift his head high — the victorious warrior refreshed after battle (v.7)

center.label: X
center.text: The LORD has sworn and will not change his mind: You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek (v.4). The center is the oath that binds kingship and priesthood together in one figure — something the Mosaic law kept strictly separate (kings from Judah, priests from Levi). This fusion is why Hebrews builds its entire Christology on this single verse. Your people will volunteer freely on the day of your power, arrayed in holy splendor from the womb of the dawn (v.3).

hebtext.note: "The LORD (YHWH) says to my lord (adoni): ‘Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet’" (v.1). THE most-quoted OT verse in the NT. Paul quotes it for Christ’s exaltation (1 Cor 15:25; Eph 1:20; Col 3:1). Hebrews quotes it seven times. The psalm establishes Christ’s session: seated at God’s right hand, ruling until every enemy is subdued. "You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek" (v.4) — Hebrews 5–7 develops this as Christ’s eternal priesthood.

The brook-drinking warrior: refreshed mid-battle, he lifts his head in victory. Seven verses. The most quoted, most christological, most theologically dense psalm in the Psalter.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: THE most-quoted OT verse in the NT. Jesus, Peter, Paul, and Hebrews all build on it. Sovereignty at 9: the Messiah’s dual office (king-priest) and his session at God’s right hand. Covenant: the Melchizedek oath is irrevocable.
